page: Microsoft Azure GS2 is an x86_64 architecture virtual machine powered by the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor. It features a dedicated Intel Xeon E5-2698B v3 processor running at 2.0 GHz, configured with 4 physical cores and 4 vCPUs. The system is equipped with 56.0 GB of system memory, yielding a high memory-to-core ratio of 14.0 GB per core. Local storage consists of a bundled 115 GB drive, and there are no integrated GPUs.

Benchmark results indicate that the GS2 is positioned on the lower end of the performance spectrum for compute-heavy tasks. Single-core and multi-core CPU performance tests, such as stress-ng and Geekbench, consistently place the server in the bottom 10% to 25% of tested hardware. Memory write bandwidth for large blocks is also limited, landing in the bottom 10%. However, the system demonstrates moderate capabilities in memory-bound tasks, with its Passmark memory score and small-model LLM inference speeds achieving average tier performance.

Given its high memory capacity relative to its processing power, the GS2 offers a specific resource density profile. While its raw compute capabilities are weak, the 56.0 GB of RAM provides qualitative cost efficiency for memory-intensive workloads that do not require high CPU throughput. Tradeoffs include lower CPU clock speeds and weak database operation performance. This server is best suited for memory-optimized workloads such as caching layers, lightweight database hosting, or memory-intensive utility tasks that do not demand high-performance computing.

Performance benchmarks indicate weak single-core and multi-core CPU capabilities, with stress-ng and Geekbench scores falling in the bottom 10% to 25%. However, memory performance remains moderate, as evidenced by average-tier Passmark memory scores and small-model LLM inference speeds. Due to its high memory density and limited compute performance, the GS2 is qualitatively cost-efficient for memory-intensive applications that do not require heavy CPU processing, making it suitable for caching layers, lightweight database hosting, and memory-bound utility workloads.
